<script>
(function(){
  function renderAll(){
    if(typeof renderMathInElement!=='function'){ return setTimeout(renderAll,50); }
    renderMathInElement(document.body,{
      delimiters:[
        {left:'$$',right:'$$',display:true},
        {left:'$',right:'$',display:false},
        {left:'\\(',right:'\\)',display:false},
        {left:'\\[',right:'\\]',display:true}
      ],
      macros:{
        '\\Sha':'\\text{Ш}'
      },
      throwOnError:false
    });
  }
  if(document.readyState==='loading') document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded',renderAll);
  else renderAll();
})();
</script>
<style>
  :root{
    --bg:#0f1218; --panel:#161b24; --panel2:#1c2230;
    --ink:#e8ecf1; --mute:#9aa4b2; --line:#2a3242;
    --yellow:#ffd866; --blue:#58c4dd; --green:#83c167;
    --pink:#e07a5f; --violet:#b39ddb; --cyan:#7de0d6;
  }
  *{box-sizing:border-box}
  html,body{margin:0;padding:0;background:var(--bg);color:var(--ink);
    font-family:-apple-system,BlinkMacSystemFont,"Segoe UI",Inter,Roboto,sans-serif;
    font-size:17px;line-height:1.65}
  a{color:var(--blue)}
  code{font-family:ui-monospace,SFMono-Regular,Menlo,Consolas,monospace;
    background:#0b0f16;padding:1px 6px;border-radius:4px;color:#f0c987;font-size:0.92em}
  main{max-width:880px;margin:0 auto;padding:4rem 1.5rem 8rem}
  h1{font-size:2.4rem;letter-spacing:-0.01em;margin:0 0 0.4rem}
  h2{font-size:1.6rem;margin:3.2rem 0 0.8rem;color:#fff;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding-bottom:.4rem}
  h3{font-size:1.15rem;margin:1.8rem 0 .6rem;color:var(--yellow)}
  p{margin:0.7rem 0}
  .sub{color:var(--mute);margin-top:0;margin-bottom:2rem}
  .widget{background:var(--panel);border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:10px;
    padding:1rem 1.1rem 1.2rem;margin:1.2rem 0}
  .widget .hd{display:flex;justify-content:space-between;align-items:baseline;margin-bottom:.6rem;gap:.8rem;flex-wrap:wrap}
  .widget .hd .ttl{font-weight:600;color:#fff;font-size:.95rem;letter-spacing:.02em;text-transform:uppercase}
  .widget .hd .hint{color:var(--mute);font-size:.85rem}
  .row{display:flex;gap:.6rem;flex-wrap:wrap;align-items:center;margin:.4rem 0}
  .row label{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem}
  input[type="text"],input[type="number"],select{
    background:#0b0f16;color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .55rem;font-family:inherit;font-size:.95rem}
  input[type="range"]{accent-color:var(--yellow);flex:1;max-width:260px}
  button{background:var(--panel2);color:var(--ink);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:6px;padding:.35rem .7rem;cursor:pointer;font:inherit}
  button:hover{background:#242c3e}
  button.active{background:var(--yellow);color:#111;border-color:var(--yellow)}
  .readout{background:#0b0f16;border:1px solid var(--line);border-radius:6px;
    padding:.6rem .8rem;font-family:ui-monospace,monospace;font-size:.92rem;color:#cbd5e1;
    min-height:1.6em;white-space:pre-wrap;word-break:break-word}
  .pill{display:inline-block;background:var(--panel2);border:1px solid var(--line);
    border-radius:999px;padding:1px 8px;font-size:.82rem;color:var(--mute);margin-right:4px}
  .note{border-left:3px solid var(--violet);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d8d2ef;background:#1a1828;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .ok{border-left:3px solid var(--green);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#d5ebc8;background:#1a2418;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  .bad{border-left:3px solid var(--pink);padding:.3rem .9rem;color:#f4d2c7;background:#2a1a18;
    margin:1rem 0;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0}
  svg{display:block;margin:0 auto;max-width:100%}
  svg text{fill:var(--ink);font-family:inherit}
  nav.toc{position:sticky;top:0;background:rgba(15,18,24,.93);backdrop-filter:blur(8px);
    border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);padding:.6rem 1rem;z-index:10;
    display:flex;gap:1rem;flex-wrap:wrap;font-size:.9rem}
  nav.toc a{color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none}
  nav.toc a:hover{color:var(--yellow)}
  aside.sidetoc{position:fixed;top:4.2rem;
    left:max(1rem, calc(50% - 440px - 220px));
    width:200px;max-height:calc(100vh - 6rem);overflow-y:auto;
    padding-right:1rem;border-right:1px solid var(--line);
    font-size:.88rem;z-index:5}
  aside.sidetoc .sidetoc-title{font-size:.7rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute);margin:0 0 .5rem;padding-bottom:.35rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line)}
  aside.sidetoc ol{list-style:none;padding:0;margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc li{margin:0}
  aside.sidetoc a{display:block;padding:.3rem .5rem;color:var(--mute);text-decoration:none;
    border-left:2px solid transparent;line-height:1.35;
    transition:color 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease}
  aside.sidetoc a:hover{color:var(--ink)}
  aside.sidetoc a.active{color:var(--yellow);border-left-color:var(--yellow)}
  @media (max-width:1180px){ aside.sidetoc{display:none} }
  @media (min-width:1181px){ nav.toc a[href^="#"]{display:none} }
  .hero{padding:3rem 0 1rem}
  .small{font-size:.88rem;color:var(--mute)}
  footer{color:var(--mute);font-size:.88rem;text-align:center;margin-top:4rem}
  table.plain{width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:.95rem;margin:.6rem 0}
  table.plain th,table.plain td{padding:.35rem .5rem;border-bottom:1px solid var(--line);text-align:left}
  table.plain th{color:var(--mute);font-weight:500}
  aside.callback{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:rgba(88,196,221,0.05);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--cyan) 45%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.callback .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.callback ul{margin:0;padding-left:1.1rem}
  aside.callback ul li{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.callback a{color:inherit}
  details.changelog{
    margin:2rem 0 1rem;padding:.6rem .9rem;
    background:rgba(0,0,0,0.22);
    border-radius:6px;
    font-size:.85rem;
  }
  details.changelog summary{
    cursor:pointer;letter-spacing:.1em;text-transform:uppercase;
    font-size:.7rem;color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);
  }
  details.changelog table{margin-top:.5rem;border-collapse:collapse;width:100%;font-size:.82rem}
  details.changelog td{padding:.2rem .6rem;border-bottom:1px dashed rgba(255,255,255,0.07);vertical-align:top}
  details.changelog td:first-child{color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);white-space:nowrap;font-variant-numeric:tabular-nums}
  aside.related{
    margin:1.2rem 0;padding:.7rem 1rem;
    background:color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 6%, transparent);
    border-left:3px solid color-mix(in srgb, var(--mute) 55%, transparent);
    border-radius:0 6px 6px 0;
    font-size:.93rem;
  }
  aside.related .ttl{
    font-size:.68rem;letter-spacing:.14em;text-transform:uppercase;
    color:var(--mute,#8c9aa6);margin-bottom:.3rem;font-weight:600;
  }
  aside.related > div{margin:.15rem 0}
  aside.related .more{color:var(--mute);font-style:italic}
  aside.related a{color:inherit}
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-begin */
  html[data-hide-widgets] .widget { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-quizzes] .quiz { display: none !important; }
  html[data-hide-lineage] .lineage-strip { display: none !important; }
  .mv-display-toggles { display: inline-flex; gap: .6rem; align-items: center; }
  .mv-display-toggle { background: transparent; border: 1px solid var(--line, #333); border-radius: 6px; padding: 5px 10px; cursor: pointer; font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1; color: inherit; transition: background 120ms ease, border-color 120ms ease; }
  .mv-display-toggle:hover { background: var(--panel, rgba(255,255,255,0.06)); border-color: var(--mute, #666); }
  .mv-display-toggle--off { opacity: 0.35; }
  /* display-prefs-css-auto-end */
</style>
<!-- display-prefs-head-auto-begin -->
<script src="./js/display-prefs.js"></script>
<!-- display-prefs-head-auto-end -->
</head>
rawBodyPrefix:
← Notebook
1 Mordell–Weil 2 Point counts 3 BSD rank 4 Regulator 5 Ш

Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer

Count points on $E$ modulo each prime and multiply. Growth of order $(\log X)^r$ recovers the Mordell–Weil rank $r$. Transpose to the $s$-plane and BSD predicts $L(E, s)$ vanishes to order exactly $r$ at $s = 1$ — a million-dollar conjecture born on EDSAC printouts. Read the story →

blocks.html:
1. Mordell–Weil: a finitely generated group of rational points

Fix $E/\mathbb{Q}$ in Weierstrass form $y^2 = x^3 + Ax + B$ with nonzero discriminant $\Delta = -16(4A^3 + 27 B^2)$. The rational solutions, with a point at infinity $O$ and the chord-and-tangent group law, form an abelian group $E(\mathbb{Q})$.

Mordell–Weil (1922/1928). For any elliptic curve over a number field $K$, $E(K)$ is finitely generated. Over $\mathbb{Q}$: $$E(\mathbb{Q}) \;\cong\; \mathbb{Z}^r \oplus E(\mathbb{Q})_{\text{tors}},$$ with $r \ge 0$ the rank and $E(\mathbb{Q})_{\text{tors}}$ finite.

Torsion is completely understood: Mazur (1977) classified $E(\mathbb{Q})_{\text{tors}}$ as one of 15 groups ($\mathbb{Z}/n$ for $n \le 10$ or $n=12$, or $\mathbb{Z}/2 \oplus \mathbb{Z}/2n$ for $n \le 4$). The rank is different: no algorithm provably computes $r$, and boundedness of ranks is open.

The proof has two pieces. Weak Mordell–Weil shows $E(\mathbb{Q})/nE(\mathbb{Q})$ is finite, via a Galois cohomology calculation. A height function $\hat h$ with $\hat h(nP) = n^2 \hat h(P)$ then descends: a quasi-basis of $E(\mathbb{Q})/nE(\mathbb{Q})$ plus finitely many small-height points generates the whole group.

The widget shows six curves of known rank and torsion. The doubling formula for $P = (x, y)$ with $y \ne 0$ is $$\lambda = \frac{3x^2 + A}{2y}, \quad x_{2P} = \lambda^2 - 2x, \quad y_{2P} = \lambda(x - x_{2P}) - y.$$

params.title: Mordell–Weil explorer
params.hint: pick a curve, double the generator

params.title: Mordell–Weil explorer
params.hint: pick a curve, double the generator

blocks.html:
Doubling $P$ on a rank-$\ge 1$ curve produces rational points with rapidly growing heights. For $y^2 = x^3 - x + 1$, doubling $P = (0,1)$ gives $(1/4, -7/8), (72/49, 611/343), \ldots$ — all free multiples of the same generator, with coordinates exploding at rate $\hat h(nP) = n^2 \hat h(P)$.

The next widget animates the full chord-and-tangent construction on a rank-$1$ Mordell–Weil group. Starting from a known generator $P$ on $E : y^2 = x^3 - 2$ (the Fermat-style curve with $P = (3, 5)$), each step builds the secant through two chosen points, reads off the third intersection with $E$, and reflects across the $x$-axis to land at the sum. Iterating $P \mapsto 2P \mapsto 3P \mapsto \cdots$ traces out the free cyclic subgroup $\mathbb{Z}\cdot P \subset E(\mathbb{Q})$ — and the rational coordinates balloon quadratically in height, the hallmark of an infinite-order rational point.

params.title: Chord-tangent iteration on $y^2 = x^3 - 2$

layout.html: Exact rational coordinates from the chord-tangent law. Denominators of $x(nP)$ are perfect squares (Mazur–Tate); the log-denominator grows like $\hat h(P)\cdot n^2$, making $P$ of infinite order and generating a rank-$1$ subgroup.

params.sectionComment: =================== Chord-tangent rational iteration on y² = x³ − 2 ===================

params.title: Chord-tangent iteration on $y^2 = x^3 - 2$

layout.html: Exact rational coordinates from the chord-tangent law. Denominators of $x(nP)$ are perfect squares (Mazur–Tate); the log-denominator grows like $\hat h(P)\cdot n^2$, making $P$ of infinite order and generating a rank-$1$ subgroup.

params.sectionComment: =================== Chord-tangent rational iteration on y² = x³ − 2 ===================

blocks.html: The free rank behaves like a lattice: once a basis is fixed, points with canonical height $\hat h(P)\le H$ grow roughly like the number of lattice points in a ball of radius $\sqrt H$, i.e. $\asymp H^{r/2}$. This gives the next geometric readout.

params.title: Height-growth heuristic
params.hint: adjust rank and height cutoff; compare predicted counts

layout.html: r = 2 H = 25

params.title: Height-growth heuristic
params.hint: adjust rank and height cutoff; compare predicted counts

layout.html: r = 2 H = 25

blocks.html:
See also
  • Group law on cubic curves
  • Mordell–Weil and invariants
Used in
Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer · Birch–Swinnerton-Dyer heuristic
Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer · Néron–Tate height and the regulator

2. Counting points modulo $p$ and the original BSD heuristic

For a prime $p$ of good reduction ($p \nmid \Delta$), reduce $E \bmod p$ to get $\tilde E / \mathbb{F}_p$, and count: $$N_p = \#\tilde E(\mathbb{F}_p), \qquad a_p = p + 1 - N_p.$$ Hasse's theorem: $|a_p| \le 2\sqrt p$, so each factor $N_p/p \approx 1$ — each prime sees about $p$ points, give or take $2\sqrt p$.

In 1963 Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer ran a pile of curves through EDSAC and plotted $$\Pi(X) \;=\; \prod_{\substack{p \le X \\ p \text{ good}}} \frac{N_p}{p}.$$ Naively each factor fluctuates about $1$, so $\Pi(X)$ should stay bounded. Instead it grew, and the growth rate tracked the Mordell–Weil rank:

BSD heuristic. As $X \to \infty$, $$\prod_{\substack{p \le X \\ p \text{ good}}} \frac{N_p}{p} \;\sim\; c \cdot (\log X)^{r},$$ where $r = \operatorname{rank} E(\mathbb{Q})$ and $c > 0$ is a constant depending on $E$. Higher rank ⇒ systematically more points mod $p$.

Heuristically, many rational points force many reductions — $a_p$ biases negative, nudging $N_p/p$ above $1$. The same data packaged as an Euler product $\prod (1 - a_p p^{-s} + p^{1-2s})^{-1}$ gives $L(E, s)$, and BSD ties its order of vanishing at $s = 1$ to this rank.

The widget computes $a_p$ by brute force — count $(x, y)$ with $y^2 \equiv x^3 + Ax + B \pmod p$ via Euler's criterion — forms $\Pi(X)$, and plots $\log \Pi$ vs $\log p$. A line fit to $\log \Pi(X)$ vs $\log \log X$ recovers an empirical slope hovering near $r$.

params.title: Point-counting product

layout.html: X = 500

params.title: Point-counting product

layout.html: X = 500

blocks.html:
Two caveats. The empirical slope drifts until $X$ is large — Sato–Tate fluctuations of $a_p$ swamp the rank signal at small $X$. And rank zero means $\Pi(X)$ approaches a positive constant, not zero: $\log \Pi$ stays flat.

See also
  • Frobenius traces and compatibility
  • Hasse bound and the Frobenius angle
Used in
Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer · BSD rank equality

3. The conjecture: analytic rank = algebraic rank

Package the local data globally. The Hasse–Weil $L$-function is $$L(E, s) \;=\; \prod_{p \text{ good}} (1 - a_p p^{-s} + p^{1-2s})^{-1} \cdot \prod_{p \text{ bad}} (1 - a_p p^{-s})^{-1},$$ convergent for $\operatorname{Re}(s) > 3/2$. Modularity (Wiles, BCDT) identifies $L(E, s) = L(f, s)$ for a weight-2 newform $f$ of level $N$, yielding entire continuation and a functional equation $\Lambda(E, s) = \pm \Lambda(E, 2-s)$ with center $s = 1$.

BSD (weak form). $\quad \operatorname{ord}_{s=1} L(E, s) \;=\; \operatorname{rank} E(\mathbb{Q}).$

That is: $L(E, s)$ vanishes at $s=1$ to the same order as the free rank. The left side is analytic (Taylor expansion); the right side is algebraic (group theory). BSD says they agree.

Refined (strong) BSD. The leading Taylor coefficient is explicitly predicted: $$\lim_{s \to 1} \frac{L(E, s)}{(s-1)^r} \;=\; \frac{\Omega \cdot R \cdot |\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})| \cdot \prod_p c_p}{|E(\mathbb{Q})_{\text{tors}}|^2},$$ where $\Omega$ is the real period, $R$ the regulator (determinant of the Néron–Tate height pairing on a basis of the free part), $\Sha$ the Tate–Shafarevich group (conjecturally finite!), $c_p$ the Tamagawa numbers at bad primes, and $|E(\mathbb{Q})_{\text{tors}}|$ the size of the torsion subgroup.

The widget sketches $L(E, s)$ near $s=1$ using toy Taylor models $L(s) \approx c(s-1)^r(1 + a(s-1))$. Toggling rank $0, 1, 2$ visibly changes the zero: a nonzero value, a transverse crossing, or a tangential touch.

params.title: BSD L-function zero
params.hint: toggle rank; watch the order of vanishing

params.title: BSD L-function zero
params.hint: toggle rank; watch the order of vanishing

blocks.html: To connect this with the refined formula, isolate the first nonzero Taylor coefficient at $s=1$. The regulator and period scale that coefficient; changing them rescales the visible tangent/parabola without altering the order of vanishing.

params.title: Leading-coefficient microscope
params.hint: fix analytic rank and vary regulator/period factors

layout.html: R = 1.4 Ω = 1.2

layout.html: R = 1.4 Ω = 1.2

blocks.html: A richer zoo: real elliptic curves from the Cremona tables span analytic ranks $0, 1, 2, 3$. For each one we can truncate the Euler product $L(E, s) \approx \prod_{p \le X}(1 - a_p p^{-s} + p^{1-2s})^{-1}$ at a moderate $X$ and evaluate near the critical point. The order of vanishing at $s = 1$ is read off by sampling $L(E, 1 + h)$ for small $h$; the algebraic rank is stored alongside as a list of explicit Mordell–Weil generators. BSD is the statement that these two columns always match.

params.title: Rank-vs-L curve zoo
params.hint: pick a Cremona curve; compare analytic and algebraic rank

layout.html: Analytic rank from sampling a truncated Euler product near $s=1$ (schematic — exact $L$-values require modular-symbol integration). Algebraic rank and generators from LMFDB / Cremona tables.

 * 389a1, 5077a1, and 27a1 (CM by Q(√−3)). Euler-factor partial products

params.sectionComment:
=================== Rank-vs-L curve zoo ===================

 * generators, leading Taylor coefficient L^{(r)}(E,1)/r!) sourced from the
 * LMFDB (https://www.lmfdb.org) entries for Cremona labels 11a1, 37a1,
 * 389a1, 5077a1, and 27a1 (CM by Q(√−3)). Euler-factor partial products
 * are evaluated on the fly with a small prime sieve; the resulting

blocks.html:
What is known (for modular $E$, so all $E/\mathbb{Q}$):

  • Kolyvagin (1989): if analytic rank $\le 1$, then algebraic rank agrees and $\Sha$ is finite.
  • Gross–Zagier (1986): for analytic rank $1$, a Heegner point gives a non-torsion element of $E(\mathbb{Q})$ whose height ties to $L'(E, 1)$.
  • Rank $\ge 2$: wide open in both directions.

BSD is a Clay Millennium Prize Problem — a \$1M bounty on the statement that two different ways of counting rational points agree. It is the arithmetic heart of the Langlands philosophy: the analytic behavior of an automorphic $L$-function encodes the arithmetic of the motive it comes from.

See also
  • Analytic continuation
  • Euler products and multiplicativity
  • Arithmetic data and modularity links
Used in
Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer · The Tate–Shafarevich group Ш

4. The Néron–Tate height and the regulator

The free part of $E(\mathbb{Q})$ is a lattice, but it needs a metric before anyone can measure its covolume. The canonical (Néron–Tate) height $\hat h : E(\mathbb{Q}) \to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$ fixes this. Starting from the naive Weil height of $x(P)$, Tate's averaging trick $$\hat h(P) \;=\; \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{h(x(2^n P))}{4^n}$$ produces a function that is exactly quadratic: $\hat h(nP) = n^2 \hat h(P)$ and $\hat h(P) = 0$ iff $P \in E(\mathbb{Q})_{\text{tors}}$.

Polarizing gives the Néron–Tate pairing $\langle P, Q \rangle = \tfrac12(\hat h(P+Q) - \hat h(P) - \hat h(Q))$, a positive-definite inner product on the $\mathbb{R}$-vector space $E(\mathbb{Q})/\text{tors} \otimes \mathbb{R} \cong \mathbb{R}^r$. The regulator is the Gram determinant on any $\mathbb{Z}$-basis $P_1, \ldots, P_r$: $$R(E/\mathbb{Q}) \;=\; \det\bigl(\langle P_i, P_j \rangle\bigr)_{1 \le i,j \le r}.$$ It is the squared covolume of the Mordell–Weil lattice, and it is the factor of refined BSD that "measures how big the infinite part of $E(\mathbb{Q})$ is."

For rank $r = 0$ we declare $R = 1$ (empty determinant). For rank $1$ the regulator reduces to $\hat h(P_1)$ for any generator. Congruent-number curves $y^2 = x^3 - n^2 x$ of rank $1$ famously have tiny heights — of order $0.1$–$0.3$ — which is why their $L'(E, 1)$ values stay small even when the analytic rank is simple.

params.title: Regulator from a basis

layout.html: 0.80 1.20
0.20

params.title: Regulator from a basis

layout.html: 0.80 1.20
0.20

blocks.html:
Two sanity checks the widget surfaces. First, the Cauchy–Schwarz constraint $\langle P_1, P_2 \rangle^2 \le \hat h(P_1)\hat h(P_2)$ holds automatically for the real pairing; violating it on a slider collapses the lattice onto a line. Second, shrinking the off-diagonal entry increases $\det G$: the more independent the generators, the bigger the regulator, and the smaller the $L$-value has to be to satisfy refined BSD.

Why this is hard. No one knows a provable algorithm to compute $\hat h(P)$ to arbitrary precision starting from the Weierstrass coefficients alone, though in practice the Tate series + descent gives any desired digit. Worse: to compute $R$ you need to exhibit a basis of $E(\mathbb{Q})/\text{tors}$, and finding that basis is the same problem as computing the rank — which is itself conjecturally solvable only via BSD.
See also
  • Mordell–Weil and invariants

5. The Tate–Shafarevich group Ш

The last mysterious factor in refined BSD is $|\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})|$. To see where it lives, consider the $n$-descent exact sequence coming from $0 \to E[n] \to E \xrightarrow{[n]} E \to 0$ and Galois cohomology: $$0 \to E(\mathbb{Q})/nE(\mathbb{Q}) \to H^1(\mathbb{Q}, E[n]) \to H^1(\mathbb{Q}, E)[n] \to 0.$$ Weak Mordell–Weil says the left term is finite, but $H^1(\mathbb{Q}, E[n])$ itself is enormous. The Selmer group $\mathrm{Sel}_n(E)$ is the subgroup of cohomology classes that become trivial everywhere locally — at every $\mathbb{Q}_p$ and at $\mathbb{R}$. It is computable, finite, and contains $E(\mathbb{Q})/nE$.

The quotient is exactly the Tate–Shafarevich group: $$\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})[n] \;=\; \mathrm{Sel}_n(E) \,/\, \bigl(E(\mathbb{Q})/nE(\mathbb{Q})\bigr).$$ Equivalently, $\Sha$ classifies principal homogeneous spaces (torsors) $C/\mathbb{Q}$ for $E$ that have a $\mathbb{Q}_p$-point for every $p$ and an $\mathbb{R}$-point, yet have no $\mathbb{Q}$-point. Every element of $\Sha$ is a failure of the Hasse principle, genuinely global, invisible to any single local obstruction.

Folklore conjecture. $\Sha(E/\mathbb{Q})$ is always finite. In the refined BSD formula its order appears as a positive integer factor: $$\lim_{s \to 1} \frac{L(E,s)}{(s-1)^r} \;=\; \frac{\Omega \cdot R \cdot |\Sha| \cdot \prod_p c_p}{|E(\mathbb{Q})_{\text{tors}}|^2}.$$ Cassels–Tate: if $\Sha$ is finite then its order is a perfect square. So the plausible values are $1, 4, 9, 16, 25, \ldots$

Known for modular $E$ with analytic rank $\le 1$: $\Sha$ is finite (Kolyvagin 1989). In small conductor tables most curves satisfy $|\Sha| = 1$, but $|\Sha| = 4, 9, 16, 25$ show up readily, and curves with $|\Sha| = 7^2, 11^2$ are tabulated. Computing $|\Sha|$ even heuristically requires descent by 2, 3, and higher, hence "$n$-descent" machinery; a rigorous unconditional $|\Sha|$ exists only in very special cases.

params.title: Selmer / Ш bookkeeping

layout.html: 16 4

params.title: Selmer / Ш bookkeeping

layout.html: 16 4

blocks.html:
The Tamagawa numbers $c_p$ appearing alongside $|\Sha|$ are much tamer: $c_p = [E(\mathbb{Q}_p) : E_0(\mathbb{Q}_p)]$ is the component group of the Néron model at a bad prime, and vanishes for $p$ of good reduction. For the modular congruent-number curve $y^2 = x^3 - 25x$, $N = 32$, one has $c_2 = 2$ and all other $c_p = 1$; $|\Sha|$ is believed to be $1$, $\Omega \approx 2.621$, $R = \hat h(P_1) \approx 0.2529$, and the refined BSD formula gives $L'(E, 1) \approx 0.331$, agreeing with numerical computation of the modular form.

Conceptually: $R$ quantifies the rational points you see; $\Sha$ quantifies the rational points you don't see but locally "should." Together with the Tamagawa and torsion fudge factors, they explain the entire discrepancy between the analytic $L^{(r)}(E, 1)/r!$ and the algebraic $\Omega$. BSD is the statement that nothing else is missing.

See also
  • Group law on cubic curves

</section>
